examples.title: Karatsuba Multiplication
examples.description: Implements divide-and-conquer multiplication for large integers
examples.code:
KARATSUBAMULTIPLY(a, b)
BEGIN
 IF a < 10 OR b < 10 THEN
 RETURN a × b
 ENDIF
 
 n ← max(size(a), size(b))
 m ← ceil(n / 2)
 
 // Split numbers into halves
 a₁ ← floor(a / 10^m)
 a₀ ← a mod 10^m
 b₁ ← floor(b / 10^m)
 b₀ ← b mod 10^m
 
 // Compute three products recursively
 z₂ ← KARATSUBAMULTIPLY(a₁, b₁) // high × high
 z₀ ← KARATSUBAMULTIPLY(a₀, b₀) // low × low
 z₁ ← KARATSUBAMULTIPLY(a₁ + a₀, b₁ + b₀) - z₂ - z₀
 
 // Combine results
 RESULT ← z₂ × 10^(2m) + z₁ × 10^m + z₀
 RETURN RESULT
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm splits each number into two halves, computes three products recursively (instead of four), and combines them using the formula. This reduces complexity from O(n²) to O(n^1.585).

examples.title: Naive Divide-and-Conquer Matrix Multiplication
examples.description: Shows the basic divide-and-conquer approach before Strassen optimization
examples.code:
MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A, B)
BEGIN
 n ← rows(A)
 IF n = 1 THEN
 RETURN A × B // Single element multiplication
 ENDIF
 
 // Partition matrices into 4 quadrants each
 // Let A = [A₁₁ A₁₂; A₂₁ A₂₂], B = [B₁₁ B₁₂; B₂₁ B₂₂]
 // Let C = [C₁₁ C₁₂; C₂₁ C₂₂] be result
 
 // Compute 8 recursive multiplications
 C₁₁ ← MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₁₁, B₁₁) + MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₁₂, B₂₁)
 C₁₂ ← MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₁₁, B₁₂) + MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₁₂, B₂₂)
 C₂₁ ← MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₂₁, B₁₁) + MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₂₂, B₂₁)
 C₂₂ ← MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₂₁, B₁₂) + MULTIPLY-RECURSIVE(A₂₂, B₂₂)
 
 RETURN [C₁₁ C₁₂; C₂₁ C₂₂]
END
examples.explanation: This naive approach requires 8 recursive matrix multiplications at each level, giving O(n³) complexity. Strassen improves this by reducing to 7 multiplications.

examples.title: Strassen Matrix Multiplication
examples.code:
STRASSEN(A, B)
BEGIN
 n ← rows(A)
 IF n = 1 THEN
 RETURN A × B
 ENDIF
 
 // Partition A and B into quadrants
 // A = [A₁₁ A₁₂; A₂₁ A₂₂], B = [B₁₁ B₁₂; B₂₁ B₂₂]
 // Represented as [A B; C D] and [E F; G H]
 
 // Compute 10 intermediate sums/differences
 A11 ← A[1:n/2, 1:n/2], A12 ← A[1:n/2, n/2+1:n]
 A21 ← A[n/2+1:n, 1:n/2], A22 ← A[n/2+1:n, n/2+1:n]
 B11 ← B[1:n/2, 1:n/2], B12 ← B[1:n/2, n/2+1:n]
 B21 ← B[n/2+1:n, 1:n/2], B22 ← B[n/2+1:n, n/2+1:n]
 
 // Compute M1 to M7 recursively (only 7 multiplications!)
 M1 ← STRASSEN(A11, B12 - B22)
 M2 ← STRASSEN(A11 + A12, B22)
 M3 ← STRASSEN(A21 + A22, B11)
 M4 ← STRASSEN(A22, B21 - B11)
 M5 ← STRASSEN(A11 + A22, B11 + B22)
 M6 ← STRASSEN(A12 - A22, B21 + B22)
 M7 ← STRASSEN(A11 - A21, B11 + B12)
 
 // Combine to get result quadrants
 C11 ← M4 + M5
 C12 ← M1 + M2
 C21 ← M3 + M6
 C22 ← M1 + M3 + M5 - M7
 
 RETURN [C11 C12; C21 C22]
END
examples.explanation: Strassen computes 7 intermediate matrices (M1-M7) instead of 8, achieving O(n^2.807) complexity. This is asymptotically better than O(n³) but has higher constant factors.
